prompt: Synthesis for kolm.ai's 2026 launch positioning. Given kolm = signed, offline-verifiable security evidence for AI agents (the buyer verifies a vendor's agent-security report themselves, no account, no server in the trust path; the wedge is a vendor-neutral 'Agent Identity Passport'), and the current site says hero 'Security evidence for your AI agent, signed and verifiable' with the 'maps to SOC 2/ISO 42001/NIST/EU AI Act/OWASP/MITRE' framing: is this TRULY the best positioning, or should it change? Give a decisive verdict (KEEP / TIGHTEN / REPLACE) with the exact recommended hero eyebrow + headline + subhead, the one-sentence category description, the single ownable wedge, and the top 5 concrete copy/positioning changes ranked by impact. Be opinionated and specific; ground every recommendation in 2026 market reality with citations.
content:
**TIGHTEN** (not keep—the current hero is solid on the core technical promise but too generic and compliance-list heavy for 2026; not replace—the differentiator is already strong and ownable).[[1]](https://www.darktrace.com/blog/state-of-ai-cybersecurity-2026-92-of-security-professionals-concerned-about-the-impact-of-ai-agents)

The 2026 market reality is an explosion of autonomous AI agents in production (Gartner: 40% of enterprise apps by end-2026), with 92% of security pros expressing concern and urgent focus on agent identity, verifiable credentials/DIDs, cryptographic attestation, runtime traceability, and compliance evidence that survives audits without shared secrets or central trust anchors.[[2]](https://www.strata.io/blog/agentic-identity/agentic-ai-governance-how-to-approach-it/)[[3]](https://www.okta.com/identity-101/improve-ai-agent-data-privacy-and-security/) EU AI Act high-risk obligations (risk management, cybersecurity, record-keeping, transparency) hit full enforcement August 2026; NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative emphasizes identification/authorization/logging; ISO 42001 provides the governance wrapper; SOC 2/OWASP/Mitre remain table stakes but lack agent-specific depth.[[4]](https://salt.security/eu-ai-act-compliance)[[5]](https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/csa-research-note-nist-ai-agent-standards-federal-framework/) Verifiable Credentials and offline-verifiable claims are emerging as the portable trust layer exactly because they eliminate servers in the path.[[6]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.02841v1)

Kolm’s offline, buyer-self-verifiable, Ed25519-signed model (no account, no server trust path) is a genuine wedge in this landscape—no dominant player yet owns “Agent Identity Passport.” The current hero underplays the portability and buyer empowerment while over-relying on a laundry list of frameworks.

### Recommended Positioning
**Hero eyebrow:** Agent Identity Passport  
**Headline:** Signed, offline-verifiable security evidence for your AI agent.  
**Subhead:** Buyers verify the report themselves—no accounts, no servers in the trust path. Cryptographic proof that maps directly to EU AI Act (Aug 2026), NIST AI RMF Agent Standards, ISO 42001, SOC 2, OWASP, and MITRE.

**One-sentence category description:** Offline-verifiable, vendor-neutral security attestations that turn AI agents into first-class, cryptographically accountable identities.

**Single ownable wedge:** The only Agent Identity Passport: cryptographically signed evidence that any buyer can verify offline, with zero ongoing server or account dependency in the trust path.

### Top 5 Concrete Copy/Positioning Changes (Ranked by Impact)
1. **Lead every page and campaign with “Agent Identity Passport” + the offline buyer-verification promise** (highest impact). This owns the exact technical + buyer-empowerment gap that 2026 standards and VC/DID discussions are converging on; the current generic “security evidence” line gets lost in the noise of 50+ AI security tools.[[7]](https://secureauth.com/solutions/use-cases/agentic-ai-governance)

2. **Replace or de-emphasize the long compliance list in the hero** with a single, deadline-tied line: “Ready for EU AI Act high-risk obligations effective August 2026.” Move the full mappings to a dedicated “Compliance Evidence” section or comparison table. Buyers in 2026 care about imminent regulatory deadlines and portable proof more than another SOC 2 checkbox.[[8]](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/)

3. **Add a crisp “Why it matters in 2026” explainer** (one paragraph or visual) highlighting agent-specific risks (prompt injection success rates >80% in red-team tests, shared-key attribution failures, multi-agent delegation chains) and how offline verification solves the “no server in the trust path” problem that centralized platforms cannot.[[5]](https://labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/csa-research-note-nist-ai-agent-standards-federal-framework/)

4. **Make the wedge explicit and repeated:** “The Agent Identity Passport is vendor-neutral by design—any seller can issue one, any buyer can verify it independently.” This directly counters the emerging identity platforms that still require integration or ongoing trust with the issuer.[[9]](https://www.dock.io/post/ai-agent-digital-identity-verification)

5. **Update secondary copy and CTAs** to emphasize buyer self-service verification (“Download the report → Verify offline with our open tool”) and contrast it against “traditional attestations that require logins or vendor portals.” This turns the technical differentiator into a repeatable sales motion grounded in real buyer pain around trust and auditability.[[10]](https://kolm.ai/security)

This tightened positioning keeps the current hero’s strengths while making kolm unmistakably own the portable, serverless verification layer that 2026 buyers and regulators will demand.
